1: môʾznê mirmāh tôʿăbat YHWH — “dishonest scales are detestable (tôʿăbāh) to YHWH.” Môʾznê mirmāh (deceitful balances) is a marketplace-ethics formula recurring across the ANE; the OT uniquely grounds market honesty in YHWH’s character rather than social convention (cf. Leviticus 19:36; Deuteronomy 25:13-15; Micah 6:11). ʾeben šĕlēmāh (accurate/complete weight, lit. “whole stone”) — completeness (šālēm) in measurement reflects divine wholeness.
2: bāʾ zādôn wayyābōʾ qālôn — “when pride (zādôn) arrives, disgrace follows.” Zādôn (presumptuous pride, arrogance) is the pride of a person who oversteps their creaturely limits. Ṣānûʿîm ḥokmāh (with the humble is wisdom) — ṣānûaʿ (humble, modest) occurs only here and Micah 6:8 (“walk humbly with your God”). Wisdom accompanies this specific form of lowliness — not servile but rightly measured.
3: tummâ yĕšārîm tanḥēm — “the integrity (tummāh) of the upright guides them.” Tummāh (completeness, wholeness, integrity) is the noun form of tōm (Proverbs 10:9); it functions as an internal guide — the integrated person is self-directing in the right path. The treacherous (bōgedîm) are destroyed by their own treachery’s perversion of their path.
4: lōʾ-yôʿîl hôn bĕyôm ʿebrāh — “wealth is of no use on the day of wrath (yôm ʿebrāh).” Yôm ʿebrāh (day of wrath/overflow) appears in Zephaniah 1:15-18 as the eschatological day of judgment; Proverbs uses the phrase for the moment when divine governance reasserts itself decisively against accumulated wickedness. On that day, ṣĕdāqāh (righteousness) alone delivers from death — echoing Proverbs 10:2.
5: ṣidqat tāmîm tĕyaššēr darkô — “the righteousness of the blameless (tāmîm) makes his way straight (tĕyaššēr).” Tāmîm (blameless, complete, whole) is the cultic-moral ideal for the person in covenant with YHWH (Genesis 17:1; Job 1:1). The straight path is not self-achieved but is the consequence of integrated righteousness; the wicked fall by their own wickedness — a self-undoing.
6: ṣidqat yĕšārîm taṣṣîlēm — the righteousness of the upright rescues them. The verse parallels 10:2 with taṣṣîlēm (rescues them) where the object is plural — righteousness is communal rescue as well as individual. The treacherous are caught by their own evil desire (ḥawwat — the same greedy-craving from Proverbs 10:3), trapped by what they reached for.
7: bĕmôt ʾādām rāšāʿ tōʾbad tiqwāh — “when a wicked man dies, his expectation (tiqwāh) perishes.” Tiqwāh (hope, a thing hoped for; lit. the string of hope) dissolves at death for the wicked because their hope was grounded in the present order. Wĕtôḥelet ʾônim ʾābādāh (the hope of wrongdoers comes to nothing) — both the wicked’s expectation and the results they counted on evaporate.
8: ṣaddîq miṣṣārāh neḥĕlāṣ — “the righteous are rescued (neḥĕlāṣ) from trouble.” Ḥālaṣ (to draw out, rescue) is a rescue vocabulary word used of divine deliverance. The wicked take the righteous’s place in the trouble — a substitutionary exchange in reverse of what is expected; the moral order eventually redirects suffering toward its true source.
9: bĕpeh ḥānēp yašḥit rēʿēhû — “through his words the godless person (ḥānēp) destroys his neighbor.” Ḥānēp (godless, profane, one who has corrupted their relation to the sacred) is a stronger term than mere wickedness — it implies deliberate sacrilege. Their speech is destructive; the righteous are saved bĕdaʿat (through knowledge/discernment) — knowledge as shield.
10: bĕṭûb ṣaddîqîm taʿălōz qiryāh — “when the righteous prosper, the city (qiryāh) rejoices.” The proverb locates moral character within communal space: the righteous person’s wellbeing (ṭûb) is civic property that generates urban joy. This is the social-capital dimension of wisdom: the righteous bless the city, the wicked degrade it.
11: bĕbirkat yĕšārîm tārûm qāret — “by the blessing of the upright the city is lifted up (tārûm).” Rûm (to be exalted, lifted up) in this civic context suggests moral elevation — the city rises through the quality of its people’s speech and character. The wicked’s mouth tears it down — the city’s fate is determined by its speech environment.
12: bāz lĕrēʿēhû ḥăsar-lēb — “whoever despises his neighbor lacks good sense (ḥăsar-lēb).” Bāzāh (to despise, treat as worthless) applied to a neighbor is the mark of heart-deficiency. ʾîš tĕbûnôt yaḥărîš (a person of understanding stays silent) — restraint is the mark of tĕbûnāh (discernment); silence in the face of a neighbor’s faults is more sophisticated than contempt.
13: hôlēk rākîl mĕgalleh-sôd — “whoever goes about gossiping (rākîl) reveals secrets.” Rākîl (a slanderer/gossip) is from a root meaning “to go about as a trader” — the gossip traffics in secrets as a merchant traffics in goods. Neʾĕman-rûaḥ (faithful of spirit) keeps confidence (mĕkassēh sôd) — covers/hides the secret rather than trading in it.
14: bĕʾên taḥbûlôt yippol ʿām — “without guidance (taḥbûlôt), a people falls.” Taḥbûlôt (pl. of taḥbûlāh) is the nautical term for the ropes used to steer a ship — plural because effective governance requires multiple steering mechanisms. Tĕšûʿāh (safety/deliverance — from the root yāšaʿ, same as yĕšûʿāh/salvation) comes with many counselors.
15: rāʿ yērōaʿ kî ʿārab zār — “whoever gives surety for a stranger will suffer harm.” The surety-for-a-stranger warning recurs in Proverbs (Proverbs 6:1-5; Proverbs 17:18; Proverbs 20:16; Proverbs 22:26-27) — financial entanglement with unknown parties is consistently presented as a wisdom failure, not just financial risk. Śōnēʾ tĕqîʿôt bōṭēaḥ (whoever refuses such pledges is safe) — the safe path is refusal of the obligation.
16: ʾēšet ḥēn titmlĕk kābôd — “a gracious (ḥēn) woman gains honor.” Ḥēn is unearned favor; the gracious woman attracts honor (kābôd) through her relational quality rather than force. Ruthless men (ʿārîṣîm) gain only wealth (ʿōšer) — the contrast of honor versus money tracks a deeper wisdom distinction between what is intrinsically valuable and what is merely economically useful.
17: gōmēl napšô ʾîš ḥāsed — “a kind man benefits himself (napšô).” Nepeš here functions reflexively (himself, his own person) rather than as soul or appetite. Ḥāsed (lovingkindness) enacted toward others returns on the practitioner. The cruel person (ʾakzārî) disturbs his own flesh (šĕʾērô) — his cruelty circles back to harm him at a visceral level.
18: rāšāʿ ʿōśē pĕʿullat šeqer — “the wicked earns deceptive wages.” Šeqer (falsehood, deceptiveness) as a modifier for wages: what the wicked earns is inherently unreliable. By contrast, zōrēaʿ ṣĕdāqāh śekar ʾĕmet — “whoever sows righteousness gains a sure (ʾĕmet) reward.” Śekar ʾĕmet (wage of truth/reliability) — the harvest metaphor for moral consequence.
19: kēn ṣĕdāqāh lĕḥayyîm — “genuine righteousness leads to life.” The adverb kēn (so, truly, genuinely) stresses the authentic quality of the righteousness that produces life — not performance but substance. Ûmĕradef rāʿāh lĕmôtô — “whoever pursues evil runs toward his own death.” The active pursuit (rādap, to chase/hunt) of evil is self-directed death-seeking.
20: tôʿăbat YHWH ʿiqqĕšê-lēb — “those with crooked hearts (ʿiqqĕšê-lēb) are detestable to YHWH.” ʿIqqēš (crooked, twisted, perverse) denotes structural distortion of the lēb (heart/will). YHWH’s delight (rĕṣônô) is in those whose way (derek) is blameless (tĕmîmê-derek) — the perfect path arising from an integrated character.
21: yād lĕyād lōʾ-yinnāqeh rāʿ — “hand to hand, the wicked will not go unpunished.” The phrase yād lĕyād (hand to hand) may describe conspiring (joining hands to plot evil) or simply emphasizes certainty (you can count on it). Wĕzera ṣaddîqîm nimlāṭ (the offspring of the righteous will escape) — the righteous person’s moral legacy extends to their children.
22: nēzem zāhāb bĕʾap ḥăzîr — “like a gold ring in a pig’s snout.” The deliberately jarring image — expensive jewelry in an unclean animal — captures the waste of beauty without discretion (ṭaʿam, taste/judgment). Physical beauty (yāpāh) is valuable but misplaced without the wisdom to use it well. The proverb is not misogynistic but is making a point about the relationship between surface qualities and character.
23: taʾăwat ṣaddîqîm ʾak-ṭôb — “the desire (taʾăwāh) of the righteous ends in good (ṭôb).” The righteous person’s desires are themselves righteous — the integration of character means that what they want is what is good. The wicked’s hope (tiqwat rĕšāʿîm) brings wrath — the word ʿebrāh (wrath/overflow) connects to Proverbs 11:4’s day of wrath.
24: yēš mĕpazzēr wĕnôsāp ʿôd — “one person gives freely and grows richer.” One of Proverbs’ most counter-intuitive economic observations: liberality (pāzar, scatter/disperse) increases, while withholding (ḥāśak from the proper amount) leads to want. The paradox of generous economics reflects a wisdom understanding of bĕrākāh (blessing) as replenishing rather than depleting.
25: nepeš bĕrākāh tĕduššān — “a generous person (nepeš bĕrākāh, a soul of blessing) will be enriched.” The phrase nepeš bĕrākāh (soul/person of blessing) is unusual — the person has become so identified with generosity that blessing is their very nature. Ûmarweh gam-hûʾ yôreh — “whoever waters others will himself be watered.” The agricultural metaphor of irrigation applied to social generosity.
26: mônēaʿ bār yiqbûhû lĕʾôm — “the people curse whoever hoards grain.” Qābab (to curse) reflects the social contract around food distribution in an agrarian community: grain-hoarding during scarcity violates communal covenant and brings collective cursing. Bĕrākāh lĕrōʾš maśbîr (blessings on the head of the one who sells) — generosity in the market is a communal blessing.
27: šōḥēr ṭôb yĕbaqqēš rāṣôn — “whoever earnestly seeks good (ṭôb) will gain favor (rāṣôn).” Šāḥar (to seek eagerly, seek at dawn — same root as šaḥar, dawn) implies an early and earnest seeking. Seeking rāʿāh (evil) brings the same evil on the seeker — the moral reciprocity principle that structures Proverbs 10-12.
28: bôṭēaḥ bĕʿošrô hûʾ yippōl — “whoever trusts (bāṭaḥ) in his wealth will fall.” Bāṭaḥ (to trust, rely on) is the key covenantal-trust verb usually directed toward YHWH (Psalms 22:5; Proverbs 37:3); trusting in wealth redirects it from YHWH to mammon. Kĕʿāleh yipraḥ ṣaddîqîm (the righteous will flourish like a green leaf/branch) — ʿāleh (fresh leaf) suggests vibrant, life-connected growth.
29: ʿōkēr bêtô yinḥal rûaḥ — “whoever brings trouble on his own household will inherit the wind.” ʿākar (to trouble, disturb — used of Achan in Joshua 7:25-26) and rûaḥ (wind, breath) — nothing to show, nothing to hold. Wĕʿebed ḥākām liḇnê-bôšet (the fool ends up serving the wise) — the social reversal: the household-destroyer becomes a servant.
30: pĕrî-ṣaddîq ʿēṣ ḥayyîm — “the fruit of the righteous is a tree of life (ʿēṣ ḥayyîm).” The ʿēṣ ḥayyîm (tree of life) links back to Gen 2-3 and forward to Revelation 22:2; Proverbs uses it in Proverbs 3:18 (wisdom is a tree of life) and Proverbs 15:4 (a healing tongue). Here the righteous person’s pĕrî (fruit, produce — their deeds and influence) is itself life-giving to others. Lōqēaḥ nĕpāšôt ḥākām (the wise wins souls) — lāqaḥ as capture/take; winning people to wisdom.
31: hēn ṣaddîq bāʾāreṣ yĕšullām — “if the righteous receive their due on earth (bāʾāreṣ).” The qal wāḥōmer (light-to-heavy argument): if even the righteous receive consequences on earth, how much more the wicked and the sinner. The verse does not say the righteous deserve punishment but that the earthly order renders judgment on all; the wicked’s reckoning is therefore assured.
